name: Vachaspati Misra
dates_notes: Polymath; dates fixed by his own colophons; commented across six darśanas.
core_thesis: Vāchaspati Miśra is the founder of the *Bhāmatī* sub-school of post-Śaṅkara Advaita, distinguished from the rival *Vivaraṇa* line by a cluster of carefully argued positions that flow from a single root commitment: *avidyā* has the *jīva* as its locus (*āśraya*) and *Brahman* only as its content (*viṣaya*). On this *jīva-āśrita-avidyā* doctrine, individual ignorances are plural, and the *jīva*, though ultimately non-different from *Brahman*, functions as the immediate seat of *adhyāsa*. Vāchaspati treats *Īśvara*'s *māyā* and the *jīva*'s *avidyā* as analytically distinct, defends *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* in a qualified form (knowledge presupposes prepared inner organs purified by *karma*), and reads *manana* and *nididhyāsana* as *prasaṅkhyāna*-like meditative cultivations producing the rise of immediate cognition. His authority extends across six *darśanas* — Advaita, Yoga, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Sāṃkhya, and Vyākaraṇa — earning him the epithet *Ṣaḍ-Darśanī-Vallabha*; within Advaita he sets the agenda the Vivaraṇins will spend centuries answering.

engaged_works.title: Bhamati
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored gloss on Śaṅkara's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya; the Bhāmatī school's founding text.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary (*ṭīkā*) on Śaṅkara's *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, named after Vāchaspati's wife and structured to follow the four *adhyāyas* of the *Sūtras* with extended *upodghāta* on the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya*. The work founds an entire sub-school: it locates *avidyā* in the *jīva* rather than in *Brahman*, treats *māyā* (cosmic) and *avidyā* (individual) as functionally distinct though both *anirvacanīya*, defends *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* in the qualified sense that *karma* produces the *cittaśuddhi* without which *śravaṇa* will not yield *aparokṣa-jñāna*, and reads *manana–nididhyāsana* as *prasaṅkhyāna*-style cultivations whose end is the rise of immediate intuitive knowledge from the Mahāvākya. The *Bhāmatī*'s long *upodghāta* on *adhyāsa* is the locus classicus for the *Bhāmatī* analysis of superimposition; Amalānanda's *Kalpataru* and Appayya Dīkṣita's *Parimala* are its principal sub-commentaries, and the *Vivaraṇa* line constitutes itself by argued opposition to its central theses.

engaged_works.title: Tattva-Vaisharadi
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Vāchaspati's mature period; relative chronology with the Bhāmatī uncertain.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Vyāsa's Yoga-Bhāṣya on Patañjali's Yoga-Sūtras.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on the *Yoga-Sūtra*–*Vyāsa-Bhāṣya* complex — the standard pre-modern *ṭīkā* on Patañjali's *Yoga* through Vyāsa's classical *Bhāṣya*. The *Tattva-Vaiśāradī* is the most influential single text in the medieval reception of *Pātañjala* yoga: it fixes the philosophical reading of disputed *sūtras* (the analysis of *citta-vṛtti-nirodha*, the structure of *samādhi*, the eight *aṅgas*, the *kaivalya*-doctrine of the fourth *pāda*), defends the Sāṃkhya-Yoga ontology of *puruṣa* and *prakṛti* as twenty-five *tattvas*, and clarifies the precise sense in which the *Yoga* school's theism (*īśvara* as a special *puruṣa*, *kleśa-karma-vipāka-āśayair aparāmṛṣṭaḥ*) differs from both atheistic Sāṃkhya and from the cosmologically-creative *Īśvara* of theistic Vedānta. For Vāchaspati's overall stature this work matters because it shows the same author who founded *Bhāmatī*-Advaita commanded the entire *darśana*-pluralism of his age — the polymathic authority by which his Advaita rulings carried weight is exhibited here in operation. Bhojadeva's *Rāja-Mārtaṇḍa* and Vijñānabhikṣu's *Yoga-Vārttika* both engage the *Vaiśāradī* as the standard reference.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Varttika-Tatparya-Tika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Uddyotakara's Nyāya-Vārttika; foundational to later Naiyāyika scholasticism.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on Uddyotakara's *Nyāya-Vārttika* — itself a commentary on Vātsyāyana's *Nyāya-Bhāṣya* on the *Nyāya-Sūtras* — establishing the standard medieval reading of the entire *Nyāya-Sūtra* commentarial stack. The *Tātparya-Ṭīkā* is foundational to later Naiyāyika scholasticism: Udayana's *Tātparya-Pariśuddhi* takes it as direct base, and the entire Navya-Nyāya tradition ultimately operates through readings of the *Sūtras* mediated by Vāchaspati's interpretation. Major doctrinal contributions include the rigorous treatment of *pramāṇa-svarūpa* and *prāmāṇya-vāda*, the analysis of *vyāpti* and the *hetv-ābhāsa*-classification, the defense of *paramāṇu*-atomism against Buddhist critiques (especially Diṅnāga and Dharmakīrti), and the analysis of *śabda-pramāṇa* in dialogue with the Mīmāṃsā. For Vāchaspati's authority within Vedānta this work matters because it positions him as a foundational figure for Nyāya scholasticism as well — the Naiyāyika opponent the *Bhāmatī* engages and the Naiyāyika tradition the *Bhāmatī* must distinguish itself from both run substantially through Vāchaspati's own *Tātparya-Ṭīkā*.
